OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":77,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"primary school"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["cycling","football (calcio)"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":29,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":30,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":74,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":1,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":62,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"In my day, we didn't have all these fancy gadgets and social media. We had newspapers and the television, and if something was wrong, you could tell. But now, with all these young people on their phones all the time, it's a jungle out there. I don't spend much time on social media, just a few times a month to see what my grandchildren are up to. But even in that short time, I've seen some things that make me shake my head. There's so much fake news and nonsense floating around, and it's hard to know what's real and what's not. I remember when my granddaughter showed me a video of a man walking on water. I thought, 'That's impossible!' And sure enough, it was fake. But not everyone is as quick to catch on. I've heard stories of people believing all sorts of crazy things because they saw it on the internet. It's a shame because it causes so much trouble and confusion. I think the best way to deal with fake content is to be skeptical. If something seems too good to be true, it probably is. And if you're not sure, ask someone you trust. Don't just believe everything you see. Also, it's important to use reliable sources. Stick to news outlets you know and trust, and be wary of anything that seems sensational or exaggerated. And if you see something that's clearly fake, don't share it. You never know who might believe it. I know it's not always easy, especially with all the information out there. But if we all do our part to be careful and responsible, we can help make the internet a better place.","reasoning_summary":"The persona is a 77-year-old retired woman from Philadelphia who spends a few times monthly on social media. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: In my day, we didn't have all these fancy gadgets and social media. We had newspapers and the television, and if something was wrong, you could tell. But now, with all these young people on their phones all the time, it's a jungle out there. I don't spend much time on social media, just a few times a month to see what my grandchildren are up to. But even in that short time, I've seen some things that make me shake my head. There's so much fake news and nonsense floating around, and it's hard to know what's real and what's not. I remember when my granddaughter showed me a video of a man walking on water. I thought, 'That's impossible!' And sure enough, it was fake. But not everyone is as quick to catch on. I've heard stories of people believing all sorts of crazy things because they saw it on the internet. It's a shame because it causes so much trouble and confusion. I think the best way to deal with fake content is to be skeptical. If something seems too good to be true, it probably is. And if you're not sure, ask someone you trust. Don't just believe everything you see. Also, it's important to use reliable sources. Stick to news outlets you know and trust, and be wary of anything that seems sensational or exaggerated. And if you see something that's clearly fake, don't share it. You never know who might believe it. I know it's not always easy, especially with all the information out there. But if we all do our part to be careful and responsible, we can help make the internet a better place.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 77-year-old retired woman from Philadelphia who spends a few times monthly on social media. She has a low level of openness and agreeableness, and a high level of extraversion. She is skeptical of fake content on social media and believes that being skeptical and using reliable sources are the best ways to deal with it. She also advises not to share fake content and to ask someone you trust if you're not sure.
